sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 61, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blue bar moves RIGHT by 4 cols, DOWN by 4-8 rows per action. UP only works once from row 16 to row 12, then stops. Blue bar is currently at rows 12-15, cols 32-51. Targets at rows 1-7, cols 36-39.
- Plan: Try moving DOWN from current position to see if UP works again from a lower row. Also check what actions happened between steps 8-20.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 60 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me look at the current state more carefully. I see:
- Blue bar at rows 12-15, cols 28-47
- A charcoal (c) object at rows 1-3, cols 36-39
- A magenta (M) object at rows 4-7, cols 36-39
- Two yellow blocks at the bottom
The targets seem to be the charcoal and magenta blocks at cols 36-39. The blue bar needs to reach them. But UP only moves from row 16-19 to 12-15, which is still far from rows 1-7.
Let me check if there's something I'm missing. Maybe the blue bar needs to be at a certain column position before UP works differently. Or maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the targets.
Let me look at the transition history more carefully to see if the blue bar ever reached rows closer to the targets.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check ALL transitions to see the full movement history
print("Full movement history:")
for i, t in enumerate(transitions):
    seg_before = t.before_frame.segmentation
    seg_after = t.after_frame.segmentation
    blue_before = None
    blue_after = None
    for n in seg_before['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b':
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            blue_before = (min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols))
    for n in seg_after['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b':
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            blue_after = (min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols))
    changed = "CHANGED" if blue_before != blue_after else "no change"
    if changed == "CHANGED":
        print(f"Step {t.before_frame.step}->{t.after_frame.step}: {t.action} | {blue_before} -> {blue_after} [{changed}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the full picture. The blue bar:
- Moves RIGHT by 4 cols per action
- Moves LEFT by 4 cols per action
- Moves DOWN by 4 rows per action (from row 16-19, 20-23, etc.)
- UP only works when at rows 16-19, moves to 12-15
- UP doesn't work from rows 12-15
The targets (charcoal and magenta) are at cols 36-39, rows 1-7. The blue bar can only reach rows 12-15 at best, which is still far.
Wait - I noticed at step 29->30, SPACE caused the blue bar to disappear (None). Then RESET brought it back. Let me look at what happened when the blue bar disappeared. Maybe SPACE is the key action to complete the level when the blue bar is aligned with the targets.
The targets are at cols 36-39. The blue bar needs to overlap with those columns. Currently at cols 28-47, it does overlap with 36-39! But it's at rows 12-15, not rows 1-7.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to have the blue bar aligned horizontally with the targets (cols 36-39), and then SPACE does something. Let me try positioning the blue bar so it's centered on the target columns and then press SPACE.
The blue bar is 20 cols wide (cols 28-47). The targets are at cols 36-39. To center the blue bar on the targets, I'd want the blue bar to span cols 36-55 or similar... but wait, the blue bar is 20 cols wide. If I want cols 36-39 to be in the middle of the bar, the bar should span cols 26-45 or 27-46 or something.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The targets are 4 cols wide at 36-39. The blue bar is 20 cols wide. If I want the blue bar to exactly cover the target columns, I need the bar to start at col 36 and end at col 55. But that seems unlikely.
Let me try a different approach: move the blue bar so it's directly under the targets (cols 36-39) and press SPACE. Currently the blue bar is at cols 28-47, which includes cols 36-39. Let me try pressing SPACE now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The blue bar is at cols 28-47, which includes the target cols 36-39
# Let me try pressing SPACE to see what happens
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
